***Hairspray*** is a 2007 [musical](/wiki/Musical_film "Musical film") [romantic comedy](/wiki/Romantic_comedy "Romantic comedy") film directed and choreographed by [Adam Shankman](/wiki/Adam_Shankman "Adam Shankman"), and written by [Leslie Dixon](/wiki/Leslie_Dixon "Leslie Dixon"). It is based on the [2002 stage musical](/wiki/Hairspray_(musical) "Hairspray (musical)") by [Mark O'Donnell](/wiki/Mark_O%27Donnell "Mark O'Donnell") and [Thomas Meehan](/wiki/Thomas_Meehan_(writer) "Thomas Meehan (writer)"), which itself was based on [John Waters](/wiki/John_Waters "John Waters")'s [1988 film](/wiki/Hairspray_(1988_film) "Hairspray (1988 film)"). Produced by [Ingenious Media](/wiki/Ingenious_Media "Ingenious Media") and Zadan/Meron Productions, the film features an [ensemble cast](/wiki/Ensemble_cast "Ensemble cast") including [John Travolta](/wiki/John_Travolta "John Travolta"), [Michelle Pfeiffer](/wiki/Michelle_Pfeiffer "Michelle Pfeiffer"), [Christopher Walken](/wiki/Christopher_Walken "Christopher Walken"), [Amanda Bynes](/wiki/Amanda_Bynes "Amanda Bynes"), [James Marsden](/wiki/James_Marsden "James Marsden"), [Queen Latifah](/wiki/Queen_Latifah "Queen Latifah"), [Brittany Snow](/wiki/Brittany_Snow "Brittany Snow"), [Zac Efron](/wiki/Zac_Efron "Zac Efron"), [Elijah Kelley](/wiki/Elijah_Kelley "Elijah Kelley"), [Allison Janney](/wiki/Allison_Janney "Allison Janney"), and [Nikki Blonsky](/wiki/Nikki_Blonsky "Nikki Blonsky") in her feature film debut.

Set in 1962 [Baltimore, Maryland](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), the film follows "pleasantly plump" teenager Tracy Turnblad (Blonsky) as she pursues stardom as a dancer on a local television dance show and rallies against [racial segregation](/wiki/Racial_segregation "Racial segregation").

Development began in 2004, with Dixon reworking Meehan and O'Donnell's first screenplay draft to tone down the musical's campiness; by the following year, Shankman agreed to direct. The musical's composer and lyricist team, [Marc Shaiman](/wiki/Marc_Shaiman "Marc Shaiman") and [Scott Wittman](/wiki/Scott_Wittman "Scott Wittman"), reworked their songs for the film's soundtrack, and also wrote four new original songs. [Principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") lasted from September to December 2006 with a budget of $75 million; filming took place on locations in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") and [Hamilton, Ontario](/wiki/Hamilton,_Ontario "Hamilton, Ontario"), and on sound-stages at Toronto's Showline Studios. Recording sessions took place in [San Diego, California](/wiki/San_Diego,_California "San Diego, California").

*Hairspray* premiered at the [Mann Village Theater](/wiki/Fox_Theater,_Westwood_Village "Fox Theater, Westwood Village") on July 10, 2007, and was released in the United Kingdom and the United States by [New Line Cinema](/wiki/New_Line_Cinema "New Line Cinema") on July 20. It achieved critical and commercial success, breaking the record for the largest opening weekend for a musical film,[[5]](#cite_note-MojoGray-5) and ranking as the seventh-highest-grossing musical film adaptation with a worldwide gross of $203.6 million.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Among its accolades, *Hairspray* was nominated for three [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards") (including [Best Motion Picture – Musical or Comedy](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Motion_Picture_%E2%80%93_Musical_or_Comedy "Golden Globe Award for Best Motion Picture – Musical or Comedy")), five [Critics' Choice Awards](/wiki/Critics%27_Choice_Awards "Critics' Choice Awards") (winning two), and the [Screen Actors Guild Award for Outstanding Performance by a Cast in a Motion Picture](/wiki/Screen_Actors_Guild_Award_for_Outstanding_Performance_by_a_Cast_in_a_Motion_Picture "Screen Actors Guild Award for Outstanding Performance by a Cast in a Motion Picture").

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hairspray_(2007_film)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

In 1962, overweight 16-year-old Tracy Turnblad lives in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"). She and her shy best friend Penny Pingleton frequently watch *The Corny Collins Show*, a local teen dance television show broadcast live on the WYZT station. Several of the dancers on the show, including lead dancers Amber von Tussle and her boyfriend Link Larkin, attend Tracy and Penny's high school. Amber's mother Velma, the station's manager, [ensures that her daughter is prominently featured](/wiki/Nepotism "Nepotism") and only allows African-American dancers to appear on the show's monthly "[Negro](/wiki/Negro "Negro") Day", hosted by local [R&B](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") disc jockey "Motormouth" Maybelle Stubbs.

Corny reveals that one of the dancers on the show will be taking a nine-month leave of absence, so Link announces auditions for a replacement to be held at the WYZT studio the next day. Ecstatic, Tracy skips class to attend the audition, but Velma rejects Tracy for both her size and support of [integration](/wiki/Racial_integration "Racial integration"). While in detention for cutting class, Tracy discovers the "Negro Day" youths practicing in the detention room and befriends Maybelle's son, Seaweed.

While leaving detention, Tracy inadvertently meets Link and dreams of a life with him. At a [record hop](/wiki/Record_hop "Record hop"), Tracy's dance moves, taught to her by Seaweed, garner Corny's attention, so he chooses her to join the cast.

Tracy quickly becomes one of Corny's most popular performers, threatening Amber's chances of winning the show's annual "Miss Teenage Hairspray" pageant the following month and her relationship with Link, who grows fonder of Tracy. When Tracy is enlisted as the spokesgirl for a plus-size fashion boutique, she persuades her [agoraphobic](/wiki/Agoraphobia "Agoraphobia") mother Edna to accompany her there as her agent, where both receive makeovers, sharply boosting Edna's confidence.

Tracy introduces Seaweed to Penny, and they become smitten. Later, Seaweed and his younger sister Little Inez bring Tracy, Penny, and Link to a party at Maybelle's store. Amber anonymously calls Edna and divulges Tracy's whereabouts. Edna rushes to the store and attempts to take Tracy home immediately, but Maybelle convinces her to stay and take pride in herself. After Maybelle sadly informs everyone that Velma has canceled Negro Day, Tracy suggests they march for integration. Link, unwilling to endanger his career, apologizes to Tracy and explains that he is scheduled to sing in front of talent scouts at the pageant.

Edna returns to her husband Wilbur's shop, but Velma gets there first and tries to seduce him. Though he remains impervious to Velma's advances, Edna arrives to see Velma forcing him into a compromising position and furiously storms out. After accusing Wilbur of infidelity and changing the locks, Edna forbids Tracy to be on the show, but changes her mind after reconciling with Wilbur.

The next morning, Tracy sneaks out of the house to join the protest, which is halted by a police roadblock. The protesters engage in a brawl, while Tracy runs to the Pingletons' home, where Penny hides her in a [fallout shelter](/wiki/Fallout_shelter "Fallout shelter"). However, Penny's staunchly religious mother Prudy reports Tracy to the police and ties Penny to her bed for "harboring a fugitive". Having been bailed out by Wilbur, Seaweed and his friends help Tracy and Penny escape. Link visits Tracy's house to find her, realizing that he loves her. Seaweed and Penny also acknowledge their love during the escape.

With the pageant underway, Velma assigns police officers to guard the WYZT studio to prevent Tracy from entering and rigs the pageant tallies to guarantee Amber's victory. Penny arrives at the pageant with Edna, while Wilbur, Seaweed, and the Negro Day youths help Tracy infiltrate the studio. Link breaks away from Amber to dance with Tracy; later, he pulls Inez to the stage to dance in the pageant.

Amber's attempt to reclaim her championship crown fails. Inez wins the pageant after a late surge of support, successfully integrating the program. Edna mans a camera to film Velma revealing her rigging scheme to Amber, resulting in Velma's dismissal. The set turns into a celebration as Tracy and Link cement their love by kissing.

## Cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hairspray_(2007_film)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cast")]

* [John Travolta](/wiki/John_Travolta "John Travolta") as Edna Turnblad, Tracy's mother who owns a laundry business, is [agoraphobic](/wiki/Agoraphobia "Agoraphobia") and ashamed of her [obesity](/wiki/Obesity "Obesity"). Travolta's casting as Edna continued the tradition of having a man in [drag](/wiki/Cross-dressing "Cross-dressing") portray the character, going back to [the 1988 film](/wiki/Hairspray_(1988_film) "Hairspray (1988 film)"), which featured [drag queen](/wiki/Drag_queen "Drag queen") [Divine](/wiki/Divine_(performer) "Divine (performer)") as Edna and at Hairspray's Broadway version, which featured [Harvey Fierstein](/wiki/Harvey_Fierstein "Harvey Fierstein") as Edna.[[7]](#cite_note-Travolta-7) Executives at [New Line Cinema](/wiki/New_Line_Cinema "New Line Cinema") originally expected the part to be filled by an actor accustomed to playing comic roles, tossing around names such as [Robin Williams](/wiki/Robin_Williams "Robin Williams"), [Steve Martin](/wiki/Steve_Martin "Steve Martin"), and [Tom Hanks](/wiki/Tom_Hanks "Tom Hanks").[[7]](#cite_note-Travolta-7) However, Travolta was aggressively sought after by producers [Craig Zadan](/wiki/Craig_Zadan "Craig Zadan") and [Neil Meron](/wiki/Neil_Meron "Neil Meron") for this role because he had starred as Danny Zuko in *[Grease](/wiki/Grease_(film) "Grease (film)")*, the second most successful movie musical to date, beaten only by *[Mamma Mia!](/wiki/Mamma_Mia!_(film) "Mamma Mia! (film)")*.[[8]](#cite_note-Fresh_Air-8)
* [Michelle Pfeiffer](/wiki/Michelle_Pfeiffer "Michelle Pfeiffer") as Velma Von Tussle, the [racist](/wiki/Racism "Racism") and [sizeist](/wiki/Sizeism "Sizeism") manager of station WYZT. Velma is invested in keeping her daughter Amber in the spotlight and *The Corny Collins Show* segregated. *Hairspray* was the first film featuring Pfeiffer to be released in five years. *[Stardust](/wiki/Stardust_(2007_film) "Stardust (2007 film)")*, also featuring Pfeiffer, was shot before *Hairspray*, but released three weeks afterwards.[[9]](#cite_note-Shankman6-9) The peculiarity of Pfeiffer and Travolta appearing onscreen together (Travolta starred in *Grease*, Pfeiffer in *[Grease 2](/wiki/Grease_2 "Grease 2")*) was not lost on the production staff;[[9]](#cite_note-Shankman6-9) Travolta requested that Pfeiffer play the part of the villainess.[[7]](#cite_note-Travolta-7)
* [Queen Latifah](/wiki/Queen_Latifah "Queen Latifah") as "Motormouth" Maybelle Stubbs, a Baltimore [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") radio [DJ](/wiki/Disc_jockey "Disc jockey") who hosts "Negro Day" on *The Corny Collins Show*. Maybelle also runs a record shop on North Avenue. Queen Latifah appeared in the successful Zadan/Meron film musical *[Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_(2002_film) "Chicago (2002 film)")*, and worked under [Adam Shankman](/wiki/Adam_Shankman "Adam Shankman")'s direction in *[Bringing Down the House](/wiki/Bringing_Down_the_House_(film) "Bringing Down the House (film)")*.[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Aretha Franklin](/wiki/Aretha_Franklin "Aretha Franklin") had been previously considered for the role.[[11]](#cite_note-Variety-11)
* [Nikki Blonsky](/wiki/Nikki_Blonsky "Nikki Blonsky") as Tracy Turnblad, an optimistic, overweight teenage girl who loves dancing. Tracy's racial acceptance leads her to become an active supporter for the integration of *The Corny Collins Show*. *Hairspray* was Blonsky's debut as a professional actress.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* [Amanda Bynes](/wiki/Amanda_Bynes "Amanda Bynes") as Penny Lou Pingleton, Tracy's best friend, a sheltered girl who falls in love with Seaweed despite the disapproval of her stern, devoutly religious and racist mother Prudy. A young actress famous for appearances on [Nickelodeon](/wiki/Nickelodeon "Nickelodeon") TV shows and in feature films, Bynes was one of the few movie stars cast among the teen roles.[[13]](#cite_note-MoviesOnline-13)

Amber becomes Tracy's enemy when she threatens both Amber's chances of winning the "Miss Teenage Hairspray" crown and her relationship with her boyfriend, Link. Snow previously worked with Shankman in *[The Pacifier](/wiki/The_Pacifier "The Pacifier")*.[[14]](#cite_note-Shankman8-14) [Hayden Panettiere](/wiki/Hayden_Panettiere "Hayden Panettiere") was also considered for the part of Amber, but was decided against in part because of her then-upcoming work with the [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") television series *[Heroes](/wiki/Heroes_(American_TV_series) "Heroes (American TV series)")*.[[14]](#cite_note-Shankman8-14)
* [Christopher Walken](/wiki/Christopher_Walken "Christopher Walken") as Wilbur Turnblad, Tracy's father, the easygoing [proprietor](/wiki/Proprietor "Proprietor") of the "Hardy-Har Hut" joke shop below the Turnblad family's apartment. [John Travolta](/wiki/John_Travolta "John Travolta") had asked that Walken be considered for the part,[[7]](#cite_note-Travolta-7) and he eventually beat out [Billy Crystal](/wiki/Billy_Crystal "Billy Crystal")[[11]](#cite_note-Variety-11) and [Jim Broadbent](/wiki/Jim_Broadbent "Jim Broadbent")[[15]](#cite_note-15) for the role of Wilbur.

The character is based in part upon [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley").[[14]](#cite_note-Shankman8-14) Efron, a popular teen actor who played Troy Bolton in the [Disney Channel](/wiki/Disney_Channel "Disney Channel") [TV film](/wiki/Telefilm "Telefilm") *[High School Musical](/wiki/High_School_Musical "High School Musical")*, was initially thought by Shankman to be "too Disney" for the role;[[14]](#cite_note-Shankman8-14) however Shankman's sister, executive producer Jennifer Gibgot, convinced him to cast Efron, believing that the teen star would draw a substantial teen crowd.[[14]](#cite_note-Shankman8-14)
* [Elijah Kelley](/wiki/Elijah_Kelley "Elijah Kelley") as Seaweed J. Stubbs, Maybelle's son, a skilled dancer who teaches Tracy some dance moves and falls in love with Penny. Kelley, a relative newcomer to film, overcame other auditioners and several popular R&B stars for the part of Seaweed.[[13]](#cite_note-MoviesOnline-13)
* [Allison Janney](/wiki/Allison_Janney "Allison Janney") as Prudence "Prudy" Pingleton, Penny's mother, a racist [Christian fundamentalist](/wiki/Christian_fundamentalism "Christian fundamentalism") whose strict parenting keeps Penny from experiencing life. [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Marc_Shaiman.jpg/330px-Marc_Shaiman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marc_Shaiman.jpg)

Songwriters [Marc Shaiman](/wiki/Marc_Shaiman "Marc Shaiman") and [Scott Wittman](/wiki/Scott_Wittman "Scott Wittman").

Music producer/composer/co-lyricist [Marc Shaiman](/wiki/Marc_Shaiman "Marc Shaiman") and co-lyricist [Scott Wittman](/wiki/Scott_Wittman "Scott Wittman") were required to alter their Broadway *Hairspray* song score in various ways in order to work on film, from changing portions of the lyrics in some songs (e.g., "(The Legend of) Miss Baltimore Crabs", "Big, Blonde, and Beautiful", and "You Can't Stop the Beat") to more or less completely removing other songs from the film altogether.

"Mama, I'm a Big Girl Now", a popular number from the stage musical, features Tracy, Penny, and Amber arguing with their respective mothers. Neither Shankman nor Dixon could come up with a solution for filming "Mama" that did not require a three-way split screen — something they wanted to avoid[[18]](#cite_note-ScriptMag-18) — and both felt the number did not adequately advance the plot.[[18]](#cite_note-ScriptMag-18)[[19]](#cite_note-Shankman3-19) As a result, "Mama" was reluctantly dropped from the film during pre-production, although it is used by Shaiman as an instrumental number when the *Corny Collins* kids dance the "Stricken Chicken". A special version of "Mama" was recorded for the film's end credits in May 2007, during the final score recording process, which featured vocals from each of the three women most famous for portraying Tracy Turnblad: [Ricki Lake](/wiki/Ricki_Lake "Ricki Lake") from the 1988 film, [Marissa Jaret Winokur](/wiki/Marissa_Jaret_Winokur "Marissa Jaret Winokur") from the original Broadway cast, and [Nikki Blonsky](/wiki/Nikki_Blonsky "Nikki Blonsky") from the 2007 film.[[20]](#cite_note-Orchestra-20) [Harvey Fierstein](/wiki/Harvey_Fierstein "Harvey Fierstein"), who portrayed Edna as part of the original Broadway cast, has a brief cameo moment in the end credits version of "Mama" as well.[[20]](#cite_note-Orchestra-20)

"It Takes Two", a solo for Link, was moved from its place in the stage musical (on Tracy's first day on *The Corny Collins Show*) to an earlier *Corny Collins* scene, although only the [coda](/wiki/Coda_(music) "Coda (music)") of the song is used in the final release print, and the song's background music can be heard immediately after the reprise of "The Nicest Kids in Town". "Cooties", a solo for Amber in the stage musical, is present in this film as an instrumental during the Miss Teenage Hairspray dance-off. As with "Mama, I'm a Big Girl Now", a version of "Cooties", performed in a [contemporary](/wiki/Contemporary_art "Contemporary art") [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") rendition by [Aimee Allen](/wiki/Aimee_Allen "Aimee Allen"), is present during the end credits.

The performance of a vintage dance called [The Madison](/wiki/Madison_(dance) "Madison (dance)"), present in both the 1988 film and the stage musical, was replaced for this version by a newly composed song, "Ladies' Choice". Portions of the Madison dance steps were integrated into the choreography for the musical number "You Can't Stop the Beat", and the song to which the dance is performed on Broadway can be heard during Motormouth Maybelle's platter party in the film, re-titled "Boink-Boink". "The Big Dollhouse", "Velma's Revenge" (a reprise of "Miss Baltimore Crabs"), and the reprise of "Good Morning Baltimore" were the only numbers from the musical not used in the film in any way.

Shaiman and Wittman composed two new songs for the 2007 film: "Ladies' Choice", a solo for Link, and "Come So Far (Got So Far to Go)", a song performed during the end credits by Queen Latifah, Blonsky, Efron, and Kelley. Another "new" song in the 2007 film, "The New Girl in Town", had originally been composed for the Broadway musical, but was deemed unnecessary and discarded from the musical. Director Shankman decided to use the song to both underscore a rise-to-fame montage for Tracy and to showcase Maybelle's "Negro Day", which is never actually seen in either of the earlier incarnations of *Hairspray*.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

One additional Shaiman/Wittman song, a ballad entitled "I Can Wait", was composed for the film as a solo for Tracy, meant to replace the stage musical's reprise of "Good Morning Baltimore". "I Can Wait" was shot for the film (Tracy performs the number while locked in Prudy's basement), but was eventually deleted from the final release print. The audio recording of "I Can Wait" was made available as a special bonus track for customers who pre-ordered the *Hairspray* soundtrack on [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes "ITunes"), and the scene itself was included as a special feature on the film's DVD release.[[22]](#cite_note-Shankman19-22)

Post-production took place in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"). Composer/co-lyricist Shaiman continued work on the film's music, employing the [Hollywood Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Hollywood_Symphony_Orchestra "Hollywood Symphony Orchestra") to record instrumentation for both the songs and the incidental score.[[20]](#cite_note-Orchestra-20)

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hairspray_(2007_film)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Production")]

### Early development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hairspray_(2007_film)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Early development")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Adam-shankman.jpg/250px-Adam-shankman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adam-shankman.jpg)

[Adam Shankman](/wiki/Adam_Shankman "Adam Shankman"), director and choreographer of the film.

Following the success of the [Broadway musical](/wiki/Broadway_musical "Broadway musical") [of the same name](/wiki/Hairspray_(musical) "Hairspray (musical)"), which won eight [Tony Awards](/wiki/Tony_Award "Tony Award") in 2003, [New Line Cinema](/wiki/New_Line_Cinema "New Line Cinema"), who owned the rights to the [1988 John Waters film](/wiki/Hairspray_(1988_film) "Hairspray (1988 film)") upon which the stage musical is based, became interested in adapting the stage show as a musical film. Development work began in late 2004, while a similarly film-to-Broadway-to-film project, [Mel Brooks](/wiki/Mel_Brooks "Mel Brooks")' *[The Producers](/wiki/The_Producers_(2005_film) "The Producers (2005 film)")*, was in production.[[23]](#cite_note-EarlyDev-23)

[Craig Zadan](/wiki/Craig_Zadan "Craig Zadan") and [Neil Meron](/wiki/Neil_Meron "Neil Meron"), the executive producers of the [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award")-winning film adaptation of the Broadway musical *[Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_(2002_film) "Chicago (2002 film)")*, were hired as the producers for *Hairspray*,[[24]](#cite_note-24) and began discussing possibly casting [John Travolta](/wiki/John_Travolta "John Travolta") and [Billy Crystal](/wiki/Billy_Crystal "Billy Crystal") (or [Jim Broadbent](/wiki/Jim_Broadbent "Jim Broadbent")) as Edna and Wilbur Turnblad, respectively.[[23]](#cite_note-EarlyDev-23) [Thomas Meehan](/wiki/Thomas_Meehan_(writer) "Thomas Meehan (writer)") and Mark O'Donnell, authors of the book for the stage musical, wrote the first draft of the film's screenplay, but were replaced by [Leslie Dixon](/wiki/Leslie_Dixon "Leslie Dixon"), screenwriter for family comedies such as *[Mrs. Doubtfire](/wiki/Mrs._Doubtfire "Mrs. Doubtfire")* and *[Freaky Friday](/wiki/Freaky_Friday_(2003_film) "Freaky Friday (2003 film)")*.[[23]](#cite_note-EarlyDev-23) After a year's deliberation on who should direct the film, Zadan and Meron finally decided to hire [Adam Shankman](/wiki/Adam_Shankman "Adam Shankman") to both direct and choreograph *Hairspray*.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Upon learning he had been hired, Shankman arranged a meeting with John Waters, who advised him "don't do what I did, don't do what the play did. You've gotta do your own thing."[[8]](#cite_note-Fresh_Air-8) Despite this, Shankman still noted "all roads of *Hairspray* lead back to John Waters."[[8]](#cite_note-Fresh_Air-8)

[Tory Gardner](/wiki/Tony_Gardner_(designer) "Tony Gardner (designer)") and his company [Alterian, Inc.](/wiki/Alterian,_Inc. "Alterian, Inc.") were hired to design and create the look of Edna Turnblad on John Travolta. Costume designer [Rita Ryack](/wiki/Rita_Ryack "Rita Ryack") wanted to put Edna into several revealing outfits, so Travolta ended up being encapsulated in prosthetics. He wore silicone prosthetics on his head and neck, and foam latex arms and legs that connected to a spandex and foam body suit.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Adaptation changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hairspray_(2007_film)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Adaptation changes")]

Dixon was primarily hired to tone down much of the [campiness](/wiki/Camp_(style) "Camp (style)") inherent in the stage musical.[[18]](#cite_note-ScriptMag-18) The 2007 film's script is based primarily on the stage musical rather than the 1988 film, so several changes already made to the plot for the stage version remain in this version. These include dropping several characters from the 1988 version (such as Arvin Hodgepile (the role Mr. Spritzer fills), Velma's husband Franklin, Corny's assistant Tammy, the [beatniks](/wiki/Beatnik "Beatnik"), et al.), removing the Tilted Acres amusement park from the story, and placing Velma in charge of the station where *The Corny Collins Show* is filmed.

One notable difference between the stage musical, the original film, and the 2007 film version of *Hairspray* is that Tracy does not go to jail in the 2007 version (thus eliminating the musical's song "The Big Dollhouse"). In both previous incarnations of *Hairspray*, Tracy is arrested and taken to jail along with the other protesters. Edna is presented in this version as an insecure introvert, in contrast to the relatively bolder incarnations present in the 1988 film and the stage musical.[[18]](#cite_note-ScriptMag-18) Among many other elements changed or added to this version are the removal of Motormouth Maybelle's habit of speaking in rhyming [jive talk](/wiki/African_American_Vernacular_English "African American Vernacular English") and doubling the number of teens in Corny Collins' Council (from ten on Broadway to twenty in the 2007 film).[[26]](#cite_note-Shankman13-26)

Dixon restructured portions of *Hairspray*'s book to allow several of the songs to blend more naturally into the plot, in particular "(You're) Timeless to Me" and "I Know Where I've Been". "(You're) Timeless to Me" becomes the anchor of a newly invented subplot involving Velma's attempt to break up Edna and Wilbur's marriage and keep Tracy off *The Corny Collins Show* as a result. The song now serves as Wilbur's apology to Edna, in addition to its original purpose in the stage musical as a [tongue-in-cheek](/wiki/Tongue-in-cheek "Tongue-in-cheek") declaration of Wilbur and Edna's love for each other.[[18]](#cite_note-ScriptMag-18) Meanwhile, "I Know Where I've Been", instead of being sung by Maybelle to the kids after being let out of jail, now underscores Maybelle's march on WYZT (which takes place in the stage musical at the end of "Big, Blonde, and Beautiful").[[18]](#cite_note-ScriptMag-18)

The song "Big, Blonde, and Beautiful" was inspired by a line that Tracy delivered in the original film ("Now all of Baltimore will know: I'm big, blonde and beautiful!"), but in the stage version and in this film, Motormouth Maybelle performs the song. A reprise of the song was added to the 2007 film, which is sung by Edna and Velma.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Pre-production and casting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hairspray_(2007_film)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Pre-production and casting")]

*Hairspray* was produced on a budget of $75 million.[[3]](#cite_note-budget-3) Casting director, David Rubin, introduced an open casting call to cast unknowns in [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta,_Georgia "Atlanta, Georgia"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), and [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago,_Illinois "Chicago, Illinois"). After auditioning over eleven hundred candidates,[[27]](#cite_note-27) [Nikki Blonsky](/wiki/Nikki_Blonsky "Nikki Blonsky"), a high school student from Great Neck, New York was chosen for the lead role of Tracy.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Blonsky auditioned for the role of Tracy Turnblad in New York City in 2006 at eighteen years old. She had no previous professional experience in acting or in singing. Blonsky had auditioned for the role because it became her dream to play the role of Tracy after seeing the musical on Broadway.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Blonsky, working at [Cold Stone Creamery](/wiki/Cold_Stone_Creamery "Cold Stone Creamery") at the time, received the news from film director, [Adam Shankman](/wiki/Adam_Shankman "Adam Shankman"), that she had received the part. Relative unknowns [Elijah Kelley](/wiki/Elijah_Kelley "Elijah Kelley") and [Taylor Parks](/wiki/Taylor_Parks "Taylor Parks") were chosen through similar audition contests to portray siblings Seaweed and Little Inez Stubbs, respectively. [John Travolta](/wiki/John_Travolta "John Travolta") was finally cast as Edna, with [Christopher Walken](/wiki/Christopher_Walken "Christopher Walken") ultimately assuming the role of Wilbur.[[7]](#cite_note-Travolta-7) Several other stars, including [Queen Latifah](/wiki/Queen_Latifah "Queen Latifah"), [James Marsden](/wiki/James_Marsden "James Marsden"), [Michelle Pfeiffer](/wiki/Michelle_Pfeiffer "Michelle Pfeiffer"), and [Allison Janney](/wiki/Allison_Janney "Allison Janney") were chosen for the other supporting adult roles of Motormouth Maybelle, Corny Collins, Velma Von Tussle, and Prudy Pingleton, respectively. Teen stars Amanda Bynes, and [Zac Efron](/wiki/Zac_Efron "Zac Efron") were cast as Tracy's friends Penny and Link, and [Brittany Snow](/wiki/Brittany_Snow "Brittany Snow") was cast as her rival, Amber Von Tussle. [Jerry Stiller](/wiki/Jerry_Stiller "Jerry Stiller"), who played Wilbur Turnblad in the [original film](/wiki/Hairspray_(1988_film) "Hairspray (1988 film)"), appears as plus-sized women's clothes retailer Mr. Pinky in this version.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Since *Hairspray*'s plot focuses heavily on dance, [choreography](/wiki/Choreography "Choreography") became a heavy focus for Shankman, who hired four assistant choreographers, Jamal Sims, [Anne Fletcher](/wiki/Anne_Fletcher "Anne Fletcher"), and [Zach Woodlee](/wiki/Zach_Woodlee "Zach Woodlee"), and put both his acting cast and over a hundred and fifty dancers through two months of rehearsals.[[26]](#cite_note-Shankman13-26)[[31]](#cite_note-Shankman11-31) The cast recorded the vocal tracks for their songs as coached by Elaine Overholt in the weeks just before principal photography began in September.[[31]](#cite_note-Shankman11-31)

### Principal photography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hairspray_(2007_film)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Principal photography")]

[Principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") took place in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), and [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_Ontario "Hamilton, Ontario"), [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), Canada from September 5 to December 8, 2006.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

*Hairspray* is explicitly set in [Baltimore, Maryland](/wiki/Baltimore,_Maryland "Baltimore, Maryland") and the original 1988 film had been shot on location there, but the 2007 film was shot primarily in Toronto because the city was better equipped with the [sound stages](/wiki/Sound_stage "Sound stage") necessary to film a musical.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-BaltimoreCommentary-34) The opening shots of the descent from the clouds and the newspaper being dropped onto the [stoop](/wiki/Urban_stoop "Urban stoop") are the only times that the actual city of Baltimore is shown in the film.[[34]](#cite_note-BaltimoreCommentary-34)

Most of the film was shot at Toronto's Showline Studios.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Most of the street scenes were shot at the intersection of [Dundas Street](/wiki/Dundas_Street "Dundas Street") West and [Roncesvalles Avenue](/wiki/Roncesvalles_Avenue "Roncesvalles Avenue"). A [PCC streetcar](/wiki/PCC_streetcar "PCC streetcar") with [Toronto Transit Commission](/wiki/Toronto_Transit_Commission "Toronto Transit Commission") livery is seen in the opening sequence. Some of the signs for the 1960s-era stores remain up along the street. Toronto's Lord Lansdowne Public School was used for all of the high school exteriors and some of the interiors, while the old Queen Victoria School in Hamilton was also used for interiors. Scenes at Queen Victoria were shot from November 22 to December 2, and the school was scheduled to be demolished after film production was completed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] As of the 2017–2018 school year, there are no plans to close this school.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Thinner than most of the other men who have portrayed Edna, Travolta appeared onscreen in a large [fat suit](/wiki/Fatsuit "Fatsuit"), and required four hours of makeup in order to appear before the cameras.[[7]](#cite_note-Travolta-7) His character's nimble dancing style belies her girth; Shankman based Edna's dancing style on the [hippo](/wiki/Hippopotamus "Hippopotamus") [ballerinas](/wiki/Ballerina "Ballerina") in the *[Dance of the Hours](/wiki/Dance_of_the_Hours "Dance of the Hours")* sequence in [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney "Walt Disney")'s 1940 animated feature, *[Fantasia](/wiki/Fantasia_(1940_film) "Fantasia (1940 film)")*.[[8]](#cite_note-Fresh_Air-8) Although early versions of the suit created "a dumpy, [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock") version of Edna,"[[37]](#cite_note-FatSuit-37) Travolta fought for the ability to give his character curves and a thick [Baltimore accent](/wiki/Baltimorese "Baltimorese").[[7]](#cite_note-Travolta-7) Designed by [Tony Gardner](/wiki/Tony_Gardner_(designer) "Tony Gardner (designer)"), the fat suit was created using lightweight synthetic materials, consisting of layered pads and [silicone](/wiki/Silicone "Silicone"), which was used from the chest upwards.[[37]](#cite_note-FatSuit-37) The suit provided the additional benefit of covering Travolta's beard, eliminating the problem of his facial hair growing through his makeup midday.[[37]](#cite_note-FatSuit-37)

### Shankman's inspirations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hairspray_(2007_film)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Shankman's inspirations")]

Shankman included a number of references to films that influenced his work on *Hairspray*:[[8]](#cite_note-Fresh_Air-8)

* The film's opening shot — a [bird's-eye view](/wiki/Bird%27s-eye_view "Bird's-eye view") of Baltimore that eventually descends from the clouds to ground level — is a combination of the opening shots of *[West Side Story](/wiki/West_Side_Story_(1961_film) "West Side Story (1961 film)")* and *[The Sound of Music](/wiki/The_Sound_of_Music_(film) "The Sound of Music (film)")*.[[8]](#cite_note-Fresh_Air-8)[[38]](#cite_note-ProducerHomage-38)
* Before we see a full shot of Tracy, we see individual shots of her upraised right and left arms. This is reminiscent of our first views of Sadie Thompson ([Joan Crawford](/wiki/Joan_Crawford "Joan Crawford")) in the 1932 film *[Rain](/wiki/Rain_(1932_film) "Rain (1932 film)")*.
* Several scenes involving Tracy, such as her ride atop the garbage truck during the "Good Morning Baltimore" number and her new hairstyle during "Welcome to the '60s", are directly inspired by the [Barbra Streisand](/wiki/Barbra_Streisand "Barbra Streisand") [musical film version](/wiki/Funny_Girl_(film) "Funny Girl (film)") of *[Funny Girl](/wiki/Funny_Girl_(musical) "Funny Girl (musical)")*.[[8]](#cite_note-Fresh_Air-8)[[38]](#cite_note-ProducerHomage-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)
* During "Without Love", Link sings to a photograph of Tracy, which comes to life and sings harmony with him. This is directly inspired from the [MGM](/wiki/MGM "MGM") musical *[The Broadway Melody of 1938](/wiki/The_Broadway_Melody_of_1938 "The Broadway Melody of 1938")*, in which a young [Judy Garland](/wiki/Judy_Garland "Judy Garland") swoons over a photo of actor [Clark Gable](/wiki/Clark_Gable "Clark Gable") as she sings "You Made Me Love You".[[8]](#cite_note-Fresh_Air-8)
* The dress that Penny wears during "You Can't Stop the Beat" is made from her bedroom curtains, which can be seen during "Without Love". This is homage to *The Sound of Music*, where [Maria](/wiki/Maria_von_Trapp "Maria von Trapp") uses old curtains to make play clothes for the von Trapp children.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hairspray_(2007_film)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Release")]

### Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hairspray_(2007_film)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Home media")]

*Hairspray* was released on [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") and [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray_Disc "Blu-ray Disc") on November 20, 2007, by [New Line Home Entertainment](/wiki/New_Line_Home_Entertainment "New Line Home Entertainment").[[42]](#cite_note-StageToScreens-42) An [HD DVD](/wiki/HD_DVD "HD DVD") version of the film was originally slated for release in 2008, but was canceled due to [New Line Cinema](/wiki/New_Line_Cinema "New Line Cinema")'s announcement that it would go Blu-ray exclusive.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hairspray_(2007_film)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Television")]

[USA Network](/wiki/USA_Network "USA Network") purchased the cable broadcast rights to *Hairspray* and was scheduled to debut the film in February 2010, but in the end, it did not broadcast that month. The film eventually premiered on July 24, 2010, with USA's sister channel [Bravo](/wiki/Bravo_(American_TV_channel) "Bravo (American TV channel)") also showing it multiple times. In February 2011, *Hairspray* made its network television debut on [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company").[[45]](#cite_note-45)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hairspray_(2007_film)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hairspray_(2007_film)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Box office")]

*Hairspray* debuted in 3,121 theaters in North America on July 20, 2007, the widest debut of any modern movie musical.[[46]](#cite_note-46) The film earned $27.5 million in its opening weekend at #3, behind *[I Now Pronounce You Chuck and Larry](/wiki/I_Now_Pronounce_You_Chuck_and_Larry "I Now Pronounce You Chuck and Larry")* and *[Harry Potter and the Order of the Phoenix](/wiki/Harry_Potter_and_the_Order_of_the_Phoenix_(film) "Harry Potter and the Order of the Phoenix (film)")*.[[4]](#cite_note-mojo-4) This made *Hairspray* the record-holder for the biggest opening weekend for a movie based on a Broadway musical.[[5]](#cite_note-MojoGray-5) This record was later broken by the release of *[Mamma Mia!](/wiki/Mamma_Mia!_(film) "Mamma Mia! (film)")*, which grossed $27.8 million on its opening weekend.[[47]](#cite_note-47) *Hairspray* is currently the twelfth highest grossing musical in U.S. cinema history, surpassing *[The Rocky Horror Picture Show](/wiki/The_Rocky_Horror_Picture_Show "The Rocky Horror Picture Show")* ($145 million) and *[Dreamgirls](/wiki/Dreamgirls_(film) "Dreamgirls (film)")* ($103 million), released seven months prior.[[48]](#cite_note-MusicalMovies-48) Ending its domestic run on October 25, 2007, *Hairspray* has a total domestic gross of $118.9 million and $202.5 million worldwide.[[4]](#cite_note-mojo-4) Its biggest overseas markets include the United Kingdom ($25.8 million), Australia ($14.4 million), Japan ($8 million), Italy ($4.6 million), France ($3.9 million) and Spain ($3.8 million).[[49]](#cite_note-49) At the time, this made *Hairspray* the third musical film in history to cross $200 million internationally, behind 1978's hit *[Grease](/wiki/Grease_(film) "Grease (film)")* ($395 million) and 2002's *[Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_(2002_film) "Chicago (2002 film)")* ($307 million).[[48]](#cite_note-MusicalMovies-48) It is the seventh highest-grossing PG-rated film of 2007, and has grossed more than other higher-budgeted summer releases like *[Ocean's Thirteen](/wiki/Ocean%27s_Thirteen "Ocean's Thirteen")* ($117 million) and *[Evan Almighty](/wiki/Evan_Almighty "Evan Almighty")* ($100 million).[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Two weeks after its original release, new "sing-along" prints of *Hairspray* were shipped to theaters.[[51]](#cite_note-51) These prints featured the lyrics to each song printed onscreen as [subtitles](/wiki/Subtitles "Subtitles"), encouraging audiences to interact with the film. On January 4, 2008, *Hairspray* was re-released in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") for one week because [John Travolta](/wiki/John_Travolta "John Travolta") was present for Q&A and autographs.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

### Critical reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hairspray_(2007_film)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Critical reception")]

The film received positive reviews upon release.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) *Hairspray* has garnered acclaim from film critics such as [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert"), *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, and *[The Boston Globe](/wiki/The_Boston_Globe "The Boston Globe")*, as well as a smaller number of reviews comparing it unfavorably to the Waters original. The film is one of the top picks on [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), with an average of 81 from 37 critics.[[55]](#cite_note-55) On [review aggregator](/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator") [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), the film has an approval rating of 92% based on 216 reviews, with an average rating of 7.8/10, making it one of 2007's best-reviewed films.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-mc07top10-57) The site's consensus reads: "*Hairspray* is an energetic, wholly entertaining musical romp; a fun Summer movie with plenty of heart. Its contagious songs will make you want to get up and start dancing".[[58]](#cite_note-58) Audiences polled by CinemaScore gave the film an average grade of "A" on an A+ to F scale.[[59]](#cite_note-59) Peter Rainer of *[The Christian Science Monitor](/wiki/The_Christian_Science_Monitor "The Christian Science Monitor")* named it the fourth best film of 2007.[[57]](#cite_note-mc07top10-57)

Ebert gave the film 3.5 stars out of 4, saying that there was "a lot of craft and slyness lurking beneath the circa-1960s goofiness", also stating that "The point, however, is not the plot but the energy. Without somebody like Nikki Blonsky at the heart of the movie, it might fall flat, but everybody works at her level of happiness ..."[[60]](#cite_note-Ebert-60) Ebert also noted that this film is "a little more innocent than Waters would have made it ..."[[60]](#cite_note-Ebert-60) Krishna Shenoi, of the Shenoi Chronicle, called the movie "Shankman's masterpiece," saying that it moved away from his previous works into a different direction, making a light comedy that deals with serious issues maturely. Shenoi also said that the film was everything he wanted *[Grease](/wiki/Grease_(film) "Grease (film)")* to be. New York *[Daily News](/wiki/New_York_Daily_News "New York Daily News")* critic Jack Matthews called the film "A great big sloppy kiss of entertainment for audiences weary of explosions, CGI effects and sequels, sequels, sequels."[[61]](#cite_note-61) *[The Baltimore Sun](/wiki/The_Baltimore_Sun "The Baltimore Sun")* review offered Michael Sragow's opinion that "in its entirety, *Hairspray* has the funny tilt that only a director-choreographer like Shankman can give to a movie", pointing out that Shankman skillfully "puts a new-millennial zing behind exact re-creations of delirious period dances like the Mashed Potato."[[62]](#cite_note-62) Dana Stevens from *[Slate](/wiki/Slate_(magazine) "Slate (magazine)")* called *Hairspray* "intermittently tasty, if a little too frantically eager to please."[[63]](#cite_note-Slate-63) Stevens noted that "Despite its wholesomeness, this version stays remarkably true to the spirit of the original, with one size-60 exception: John Travolta as Edna Turnblad", saying "How you feel about *Hairspray* will depend entirely on your reaction to this performance ..."[[63]](#cite_note-Slate-63)

*[The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")*'s [David Denby](/wiki/David_Denby_(film_critic) "David Denby (film critic)") felt the new version of *Hairspray* was "perfectly pleasant", but compared unfavorably to the Broadway musical, since "[director Adam Shankman and screenwriter Leslie Dixon] have removed the traces of camp humor and Broadway blue that gave the stage show its happily knowing flavor."[[64]](#cite_note-DavidDenby-64) Denby criticized the dance numbers, calling them "unimaginatively shot," and he considered "the idea of substituting John Travolta for Harvey Fierstein as Tracy's hefty mother ... a blandly earnest betrayal."[[64]](#cite_note-DavidDenby-64) Stephanie Zacharek of *[Salon.com](/wiki/Salon.com "Salon.com")* found *Hairspray* "reasonably entertaining. But do we really need to be entertained reasonably? Waters' original was a crazy sprawl that made perfect sense; this *Hairspray* toils needlessly to make sense of that craziness, and something gets lost in the translation."[[65]](#cite_note-Zacharek-65) Zacharek was also displeased with the way Latifah's performance of "I Know Where I've Been" was incorporated into the movie, saying "The filmmakers may believe they're adding an extra layer of seriousness to the material ... [but] the inclusion of this big production number only suggests that the filmmakers fear the audience won't get the movie's message unless it's spelled out for them."[[65]](#cite_note-Zacharek-65)

Despite critical and commercial success, *Hairspray* garnered some criticism upon its release from the [LGBT community](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT"), particularly Travolta's portrayal of Edna Turnblad, a role played in the original film by drag performer [Divine](/wiki/Divine_(actor) "Divine (actor)"), and in the stage adaptation by [Harvey Fierstein](/wiki/Harvey_Fierstein "Harvey Fierstein"). Kevin Naff, a managing editor for a [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")/Baltimore area gay newspaper named the *[Washington Blade](/wiki/Washington_Blade "Washington Blade")*, called for a boycott of the film, alleging that [Scientology](/wiki/Scientology "Scientology"), in which Travolta is an adherent, was [homophobic](/wiki/Homophobic "Homophobic"), and it supported ["cure" workshops for homosexuals](/wiki/Ex-gay "Ex-gay").[[66]](#cite_note-66) Adam Shankman rebuffed Naff's proposed boycott stating that Travolta was not a homophobe, as he (Shankman), Waters, Shaiman, Wittman, and several other crew and creative staff were homosexual, and Travolta got along well with the entire production.[[67]](#cite_note-ShankmanOnBoycott-67) Shankman made it clear "John's personal beliefs did not walk onto my set. I never heard the word 'Scientology'."[[67]](#cite_note-ShankmanOnBoycott-67)

## Cancelled sequel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hairspray_(2007_film)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Cancelled sequel")]

Due to *Hairspray*'s financial success, [New Line Cinema](/wiki/New_Line_Cinema "New Line Cinema") had asked [John Waters](/wiki/John_Waters_(filmmaker) "John Waters (filmmaker)") to write a [sequel](/wiki/Sequel "Sequel") to the film.[[90]](#cite_note-Sequel-91) Waters reunited with director/choreographer [Adam Shankman](/wiki/Adam_Shankman "Adam Shankman") for the project, and songwriters [Marc Shaiman](/wiki/Marc_Shaiman "Marc Shaiman") and [Scott Wittman](/wiki/Scott_Wittman "Scott Wittman") were set to compose the film's musical numbers.[[90]](#cite_note-Sequel-91)[[91]](#cite_note-92)

The story would have looked at Tracy's entering the late 1960s era of music and the [British Invasion](/wiki/British_Invasion "British Invasion"),[[92]](#cite_note-93) and used the [Hippie](/wiki/Hippie "Hippie") movement and [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") as backdrops.[[93]](#cite_note-94) While no official casting was announced, New Line said that they hoped to "snag much of the original *Hairspray* cast."[[90]](#cite_note-Sequel-91) [John Travolta](/wiki/John_Travolta "John Travolta"), however, publicly announced that he would not return because he is "not a big sequel guy".[[94]](#cite_note-95)

The sequel was set for a mid-July 2010 release by [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros."), which owns New Line Cinema.[[90]](#cite_note-Sequel-91) However, in June 2010, Shankman told British press that *Hairspray 2: White Lipstick* was no longer in development.[[95]](#cite_note-96) Shankman has also said that there will be no sequel.[[96]](#cite_note-97) In February 2019, [John Waters](/wiki/John_Waters "John Waters") announced he had written a sequel for [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO"), but did not produce it.[[97]](#cite_note-98)
